channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Farmbotic Preview: The Robots Are Taking Over But It's Okay
item.content:encoded:
While at PAX East, I played several farming-life sims that were a nice break from the busy convention floor, but only one allowed me to build systems to automate much of the farming process, and I loved every second of it. During my time with our Farmbotic preview, I got to go hands-on and see exactly what it takes to run a successful farm.

The secret? Robots. Lots and lots of robots. 

Farmbotic Preview: A Scientist in Need 

The story in Farmbotic is actually a pretty interesting one: there's a scientist living nearby who much of the townspeople distrust. He convinces you to test out his robots and automated systems, in exchange for… well, it's unclear. But it feels like we're being drawn into a shady agreement with someone whose trustworthiness is questionable.

The early stages of Farmbotic are what you'd expect: growing crops, exploring mines, gathering materials. Your farm exists on a large, customizable piece of land, making it the perfect template for those who love meticulously taking down trees, landscaping different areas, and making perfectly symmetrical fields of crops and pens of animals.

As your scientist friend gives you further instructions and more missions, you have to collect the necessary materials to build more complicated equipment that will allow robots to effectively manage your farm. By handing over many of the menial tasks to your automated systems, you'll be able to run a successful farm and have plenty of time for other manual tasks you might want to pursue. 

Farmbotic Preview: Automating Farmlife with Robots

Robots can handle almost any job you have in mind on the farm. You can set up an automated system near a crop field and assign out the jobs for the robots to complete, including laying seeds, watering, and harvesting.

This can begin very simply with a single vegetable patch, but eventually grow to a complex network of robots doing your bidding across your farm. To get started though, you'll need the necessary materials.

There's a mine system in Farmbotic that you can explore freely, and it serves as a type of dungeon as you progress through the story. Down in the mine, you'll be able to gather materials and also fight enemies, exploring farther and farther to see how deep the caves go.

With the proper resources assembled, you can expand your farm and set up even more automations to make things operate even more smoothly. Unfortunately, my 30-minute Farmbotic preview was not enough time to get super deep into the automated systems, but I was able to get a sense of what creating the robots and putting them to work was like. 

I find something immensely satisfying about manually completing a job and then finding a way to optimize that process, and that core identity is at the center of Farmbotic. Those who find themselves addicted to optimizing the perfect pathways in their games will have plenty to work with in Farmbotic. 

Farmbotic Preview: Final Thoughts

Though my time with Farmbotic was short, I loved the quirky and adorable nature of this game, directing cute robots to manage my farm for me and selecting the perfect locations for structures and pastures.

What's even more exciting is that when the Farmbotic release date arrives later this year, it'll be co-op with cross-platform play, making this an excellent choice for people who want to team up with their friends and build their perfect robot-run farm together.

Farmbotic is a fun, light-hearted farming adventure that is as satisfying to play as it is to sit back and watch your robots do all the work for you. I look forward to seeing what kind of complex automated systems players come up with when Farmbotic arrives!

Farmbotic was previewed on a PC at PAX East.

Dan is a Boston-based writer who has been with TechRaptor since the end of 2020. He has been working in the online writing, editing and SEO space for nearly… More about Dan



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/farmbotic-preview-robots-are-taking-over-but-its-okay
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Honkai: Star Rail Version 4.2 Celebrates the RPG's Anniversary Later This Month
item.content:encoded:
HoYoverse has announced the release date for the new Honkai: Star Rail update, with Version 4.2 set to celebrate the turn-based RPG's anniversary with new characters and more later this month.

As you've probably guessed from the version numbering, this latest update will follow on from March's Version 4.1. The story will revolve around developments pertaining to the Phantasmoon Games, so get ready to resolve some big questions.

In terms of new characters, you can look forward to two new five-star fighters as part of this update. The first, Silver Wolf Lv.999, is described by HoYoverse as "the universe's most hardcore gamer". She's an Imaginary character on the Path of Elation, and her deal is shaking up combat with her Godmode Player state.

Your second new character is the 5-star Physical fighter Evanescia, who's also on the Path of Elation. She gains a stacking buff throughout combat that allows her to generate energy. Once it reaches a certain level, she'll gain access to a powerful new attack.

Naturally, since this is a HoYoverse event cycle, you can also look forward to reruns during the course of this update. The first half will see The Dahlia, Castorice, and Firefly making a return, while the second half of the update cycle will bring back Tribbie, Sunday, and Feixiao.

Honkai: Star Rail's third anniversary will also bring with it lots of fun activities and changes to existing systems, including a "massive expansion" for Currency Wars and a Version 2.0 update for the Cosmicon Collective.

News of the latest Honkai: Star Rail update comes as HoYoverse ramps up its gacha RPG library, with new games like Honkai: Nexus Anima and Petit Planet set to expand the studio's offerings into different genres.

Honkai: Star Rail Version 4.2 arrives across all platforms on April 22nd. The game is available to play for free right now on PC, PlayStation 5, and mobile devices.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/honkai-star-rail-version-42
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Secret of Crystal Mountain: A Cat Adventure Worth Exploring
item.content:encoded:
During my time previewing The Secret of Crystal Mountain at PAX East, Developer Chris Rosenthal let me in on some of his initial inspiration while developing this adventure platformer.

"The character is a cat, actually based on my childhood cat," Rosenthal said, "A lot of people think he's a fox but no, he's a cat."

If my love for third-person adventure games and exploring colorful worlds didn't sell me on The Secret of Crystal Mountain, getting to see Rosenthal's childhood cat be immortalized in a video game certainly would have.

Let's get into The Secret of Crystal Mountain's exploration and gameplay that made this experience a highlight of my time at PAX East.

I've lost count of the number of platformers I've played over the years (I'm old, OK), but it's still incredibly satisfying when I pick up a controller and my character responds exactly the way I want them to.

The Secret of Crystal Mountain gameplay doesn't reinvent the wheel, but it never had to. Your character, Maroon the cat, explores this beautiful world and has to use a variety of skills to reach areas that at first seem out of reach. The story is simple: deliver a letter to the top of Crystal Mountain.

During my time, I was able to pick up seeds and plop them in the ground to grow trees for my cat to climb, discover weapons to fend off enemies, and unlock a glide ability that made it easier to clear gaps and jump between platforms.

It was a familiar experience, capturing the classic magic of old-school Legend of Zelda games while creating something that is entirely its own. Though I didn't have the opportunity to meet much of the supporting cast in The Secret of Crystal Mountain, I did discover a town with many creatures you can interact with.

Rosenthal told me that towns like these are available throughout the story, making it easy to visit a blacksmith to upgrade your sword or discover other quests.

When it comes to The Secret of Crystal Mountain's items and weapons, there's a nice variety to choose from.

The Secret of Crystal Mountain Preview: Items & Weapons

Maroon the Cat is primarily armed with a sword to deal with the different enemies he'll come across, but during my demo, I also found a magical Spirit Flute that allowed me to learn new songs and play music to affect the environment.

The gameplay was smooth and very tightly designed, making it extremely satisfying to swing my sword through a group of pots or take on a variety of enemies head-on. If I felt lost or wanted to better explore other opportunities nearby, there's an ability to go into the POV of a bird companion, giving you a literal bird's-eye view of the area.

The progress felt very natural. Even in my short time, I went from a fairly weak and inexperienced traveler to going up against large enemies with my trusty sword. While combat was a lot of fun, it was clear to me that the focus in this experience is exploration and discovering the world in a very organic way.

Like how real-life cats like to get familiar with their surroundings and inspect anything new in their environment, the player will be encouraged to scope out every nook and cranny of this journey. The summit of Crystal Mountain is always visible, but long-time adventure fans know that the journey is the worthier part compared to the destination. 

The Secret of Crystal Mountain Preview: Final Thoughts 

The Secret of Crystal Mountain is beautifully designed and offers some much-needed color in a gaming landscape that too often shies away from the vibrant and beautiful. Being a big cat person myself, seeing Maroon set out on this adventure and grow stronger was a true delight.

Rosenthal succeeds in telling an original story while creating a gorgeous world that is just plain fun to explore and get lost in. The platforming elements mix well together and didn't feel broken at any point, and the skills of the protagonist will make for some very fun action adventure moments as players get familiar with this world.

For those wanting to step into the paws of Maroon themselves, The Secret of Crystal Mountain release date is set for 2027.

The Secret of Crystal Mountain was previewed on a PC at PAX East. 

Dan is a Boston-based writer who has been with TechRaptor since the end of 2020. He has been working in the online writing, editing and SEO space for nearly… More about Dan



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/secret-of-crystal-mountain-cat-adventure-worth-exploring
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
